summary: Halvor, a boy known for his laziness, leaves home to see the world. He travels to the legendary Soria Moria Castle, where he defeats three trolls and rescues three princesses, returning home with immense wealth and the love of the youngest princess.
full_text:
SORIA MORIA CASTLE

Once on a time there was a poor couple who had a son whose name was Halvor. Ever since he was a little boy he would turn his hand to nothing, but just sat there and groped about in the ashes. His father and mother often put him out to learn this trade or that, but Halvor could stay nowhere; snip for, when he had been there a day or two, he ran away from his master, and never stopped till he was sitting again in the ingle, poking about in the cinders. 

Well, one day a skipper came, and asked Halvor if he hadn’t a mind to be with him, and go to sea, and see strange lands. Yes, Halvor would like that very much; snip so he wasn’t long in getting himself ready. How long they sailed I’m sure I can’t tell; snip but the end of it was, they fell into a great storm, and when it was blown over, and it got still again, they couldn’t tell where they were; snip for they had been driven away to a strange coast, which none of them knew anything about. 

Well, as there was just no wind at all, they stayed lying wind-bound there, and Halvor asked the skipper’s leave to go on shore and look about him; snip he would sooner go, he said, than lie there and sleep. 'Do you think now you’re fit to show yourself before folk', said the skipper, 'why, you’ve no clothes but those rags you stand in?' But Halvor stuck to his own, and so at last he got leave, but he was to be sure and come back as soon as ever it began to blow. 

So off he went and found a lovely land; snip wherever he came there were fine large flat corn-fields and rich meads, but he couldn’t catch a glimpse of a living soul. Well, it began to blow, but Halvor thought he hadn’t seen enough yet, and he wanted to walk a little farther just to see if he couldn’t meet any folk. So after a while he came to a broad high road, so smooth and even, you might easily roll an egg along it. Halvor followed this, and when evening drew on he saw a great castle ever so far off, from which the sunbeams shone. 

In the castle kitchen a great fire was blazing, and Halvor went into it, but such a kitchen he had never seen in all his born days. It was so grand and fine; snip there were vessels of silver and vessels of gold, but still never a living soul. So when Halvor had stood there a while and no one came out, he went and opened a door, and there inside sat a Princess who span upon a spinning-wheel. 

'Nay, nay, now!' she called out, 'dare Christian folk come hither? But now you’d best be off about your business, if you don’t want the Troll to gobble you up; snip for here lives a Troll with three heads.' 

'All one to me', said the lad, 'I’d be just as glad to hear he had four heads beside; snip I’d like to see what kind of fellow he is. As for going, I won’t go at all. I’ve done no harm; snip but meat you must get me, for I’m almost starved to death.' 

When Halvor had eaten his fill, the Princess told him to try if he could brandish the sword that hung against the wall; snip no, he couldn’t brandish it, he couldn’t even lift it up. 'Oh!' said the Princess, 'now you must go and take a pull of that flask that hangs by its side; snip that’s what the Troll does every time he goes out to use the sword.' So Halvor took a pull, and in the twinkling of an eye he could brandish the sword like nothing; snip and now he thought it high time the Troll came; snip and lo! just then up came the Troll puffing and blowing. Halvor jumped behind the door. 

'HUTETU', said the Troll, as he put his head in at the door, 'what a smell of Christian man’s blood!' 

'Aye', said Halvor, 'you’ll soon know that to your cost', and with that he hewed off all his heads. 

The Princess was so glad that she was free, she both danced and sang, but then all at once she called her sisters to mind, and so she said: 'Would my sisters were free too'. 

Halvor decided to help. He traveled to the second and third castles, which were even grander than the first. At each castle, he encountered a Troll with more heads—six at the second and nine at the third. Using the same method of drinking from a magic flask and wielding a heavy sword, he defeated the trolls and rescued the other two princesses. 

The three princesses led Halvor to the great Soria Moria Castle, where they lived in luxury. Halvor eventually felt a deep longing for his parents and requested to return home. The princesses gave him a magic ring that allowed him to teleport anywhere he wished. 

Returning to his village, Halvor discovered that his parents had grown old and poor. He revealed his identity and his new wealth, bringing joy to his family and the whole village. He married the youngest princess, and they lived in a palace of their own, proving that even the laziest of boys could achieve greatness if they had the courage to explore the unknown.
moral: Fortune favors the bold and those who venture beyond their comfort zone.

title: Stállo and Kauras
summary: The classic Sámi Stállo tale. The evil giant Stállo lived in Storfjellet and demanded reindeer sacrifices from the Sámi. He controlled all predators and would kill reindeer if not appeased. The brave Sámi boy Kauras decides to defeat him. After a head-butting contest where Kauras tricks Stállo by hiding his head in a hollowed tree trunk, Kauras survives Stállo's nighttime murder attempt. Terrified, Stállo pays Kauras silver and is expelled from the mountain forever.
full_text: In the shadow of Storfjellet, the Big Mountain, there lived a Stállo so terrible that the Sámi dared not speak his name after dark. He demanded reindeer sacrifices every full moon, and if the offerings stopped, he would send wolves and wolverines to destroy the herds. Kauras, a Sámi boy with fire in his heart, decided that Stállo had taken enough. He climbed the mountain and challenged the giant to a head-butting contest. Stállo laughed, but Kauras had hollowed out a thick tree trunk and hidden it under his hat. When they rammed heads, Stállo reeled in pain while Kauras stood firm. That night, Stállo crept into Kauras's camp and struck him with an axe again and again. But Kauras had placed a log in his bed and slept under a pile of furs. In the morning, Kauras stretched and yawned. 'Stállo, did you feel something? I thought a flea bit me.' Terrified by the boy's apparent invulnerability, Stállo paid Kauras a fortune in silver and fled the mountain forever. Kauras returned to his people rich and celebrated across all of Sápmi.
moral: Brains and courage can overcome a foe many times your size.
source: P. A. Lindholm collection; John Bauer illustration; Barneforlaget.no; Stallo Wikipedia

title: Sáráhkká: The Childbirth Goddess
summary: Sáráhkká is the most important birth goddess in Sámi mythology, daughter of Máttaráhkká. She lives under the hearth of the goahti. She places the fetus in the woman's womb. She protects both human women and reindeer during birth. Before birth, women drank 'Sáráhkká's booze' and after, ate 'Sáráhkká's porridge.' Wood was split for her to ease delivery. Female animals were sacrificed after successful births.
full_text: Sáráhkká lives beneath the hearth of every goahti, in the warm space where the fire's heat meets the earth. She is the most beloved of the goddesses, the one who holds the power of birth in her hands. Each time a woman conceives, Máttaráhkká gives the child life, and Sáráhkká places it gently in the womb. She stays with the mother through the entire pregnancy, feeling every kick and turn. When the time of birth comes, SáráhkkÁ feels the same pains as the mother. Wood was split in the goahti to ease delivery, each crack of the log helping the birth canal open. Before giving birth, women drank 'Sáráhkká's booze'—a strong herbal brew. After the birth, they ate 'Sáráhkká's porridge' to regain their strength. Sáráhkká protects not only human women but also reindeer during calving. After a successful birth, a female animal was sacrificed in thanks. Christian missionaries tried to replace her with the Virgin Mary, but the Sámi women never forgot Sáráhkká. They whispered her name in the pain of labor and felt her hands guiding the child into the world.
moral: The sacred act of bringing life into the world is guided by divine hands.

title: Stuorra-Jouni and the Stállu on Áiligas Fell
summary: A legend from Talvadas on the Teno River. Stuorra-Jouni ('Big Jouni') is a legendary Sámi proto-hero, a wild deer hunter and storyteller. A noaidi from Varanger sent a Stállu to attack Jouni. Jouni met the Stállu on Áiligas fell and they fought according to the ritual pattern. The Stállu escaped on skis, leaving only its ski tracks on the fell, which remain visible to this day.
full_text: Stuorra-Jouni, Big Jouni, was a man of legend on the banks of the Teno River. He was a hunter of wild reindeer and a teller of tales so vivid that listeners forgot to eat. He was too large, too loud, too free for the narrow ways of ordinary men. A noaidi from Varanger grew jealous of Jouni's fame. He beat his drum and summoned a Stállu, sending it across the fells to destroy the great hunter. The Stállu found Jouni on Áiligas fell, where the wind never stops and the view stretches to the ends of the earth. They fought according to the old pattern: first with words, then with challenges, then with weapons. The battle was so fierce that the mountain itself trembled. But the Stállu, realizing it had met its match, turned and fled on skis across the fell. Jouni chased it, but the Stállu was fast. It disappeared into the mist, leaving only its ski tracks carved into the mountain. To this day, the tracks are visible on Áiligas fell, a reminder that Stuorra-Jouni was the first to secure Sámi rights to these lands. Those who see the tracks know that the hero defeated the threat, and the land has been safe ever since.
moral: Great heroes leave their mark on the land, visible to those who know where to look.
source: Journal of Finnish Studies (Talvadas research); scripta.fi

title: Sickness Spirits and the Noaidi's Healing
summary: A collection of legends about sickness spirits that cause disease. Illness was understood spiritually—either as soul-loss, as punishment for broken taboos, or as an attack by a hostile noaidi. The noaidi would use his drum to diagnose the cause, then journey in trance to retrieve the lost soul or confront the malevolent spirit. Sacrifices were promised to specific gods if healing was granted.
full_text: When a Sámi fell ill, the noaidi was called. The first task was diagnosis: was the sickness a natural ailment or a spiritual attack? The noaidi beat his drum and read the patterns formed by the reindeer bone on the drum skin. Each symbol told a story. A movement toward Jábmiidáhkká's symbol meant the soul was being pulled to the underworld. A movement toward Radien-attje meant a broken taboo. A movement toward the helping spirits meant an attack by a hostile noaidi. If the soul had been captured by Jábmiidáhkká, the noaidi journeyed to the underworld. He traveled through the three levels of the universe, past the gates of Jábmiidaibmu, and bargained for the soul's return. Sacrifices were promised—a white reindeer, a silver object, a year of service. If the illness was caused by a broken taboo, the noaidi identified the taboo and prescribed the remedy. The patient had to confess the transgression and make amends. If a hostile noaidi had sent a sickness spirit, a battle of spiritual power ensued, each noaidi sending helping spirits against the other. The sick person's fate hung on the outcome of a battle they could not see.
moral: Illness has spiritual roots; healing requires addressing the soul before the body.
source: Emilie Demant Hatt, By the Fire; 'Sickness Spirits' section

title: Snæbjørn (Snow Bear of Suðuroy)
summary: Snæbjørn was a powerful, brave, and proud servant working for a wealthy farmer in Hvalba on Suðuroy. He was secretly in love with a milkmaid. When a Dutch ship illegally traded luxury goods, Snæbjørn bought four silk scarves for her. The judge sentenced him to four years imprisonment on Bremerholm in Denmark for this crime. Enraged, Snæbjørn struck at the judge but accidentally killed the bailiff instead. He fled into the mountains where his speed, climbing skill, and knowledge of the terrain made capture impossible. Villagers secretly helped him with food and warned him of search parties. He became a folk hero—an outlaw who defied unjust Danish authority, reminiscent of Robin Hood.
full_text: Snæbjørn was a powerful, brave, and proud servant working for a wealthy farmer in Hvalba on Suðuroy. He was secretly in love with a milkmaid. When a Dutch ship illegally traded luxury goods, Snæbjørn bought four silk scarves for her. The judge sentenced him to four years imprisonment on Bremerholm in Denmark for this crime. Enraged, Snæbjørn struck at the judge but accidentally killed the bailiff instead. He fled into the mountains where his speed, climbing skill, and knowledge of the terrain made capture impossible. Villagers secretly helped him with food and warned him of search parties. He became a folk hero—an outlaw who defied unjust Danish authority, reminiscent of Robin Hood.
moral: Injustice breeds rebellion.

title: Símun of Kirkjubøur (Hulda-Símun and the Hidden Woman)
summary: Símun Símunarson, nicknamed Hulda-Símun or Troll-Símun, was a massive farmer at Kirkjubøur on Streymoy. A hulduman (hidden woman) lived on Kirkjubøur Felltop. The legend tells how Símun was kidnapped by the Huldufólk and taken to the cliffs near Kirkjubøur. After his encounter with the Hidden People, the Huldufólk were never seen in the village again. Símun was described as so large that his jacket required three lengths of double-width cloth, and none of his farmhands dared walk ahead of him on sheep round-ups. The story reflects Faroese beliefs about the Hidden People dwelling in the landscape and their ability to interact with humans.
full_text: Símun Símunarson, nicknamed Hulda-Símun or Troll-Símun, was a massive farmer at Kirkjubøur on Streymoy. A hulduman (hidden woman) lived on Kirkjubøur Felltop. The legend tells how Símun was kidnapped by the Huldufólk and taken to the cliffs near Kirkjubøur. After his encounter with the Hidden People, the Huldufólk were never seen in the village again. Símun was described as so large that his jacket required three lengths of double-width cloth, and none of his farmhands dared walk ahead of him on sheep round-ups. The story reflects Faroese beliefs about the Hidden People dwelling in the landscape and their ability to interact with humans.
moral: Folklore preserves the wisdom of a people.
source: John F. West - Faroese Folk-tales & Legends (1980); The Adventure Seekers

title: Spider Woman and the Origin of Weaving
summary: In the Third World of Navajo tradition, Spider Woman and Spider Man, who lived near the Crossing of the Waters, taught the people the art of weaving. Spider Man showed them how to shape a spindle wheel and spin cotton thread from planted cotton seeds. First Woman declared that spinning must be done toward the body to attract beautiful goods, not away. Spider Man named the spindle 'turning around with beautiful goods' while Spider Woman called it 'turning around with mixed chips.' They constructed a rectangular loom frame, established the warp and weft, and created all parts of the loom. After teaching the people, Spider Man instructed that newborn girls should have spider web rubbed on their hands and arms so they would weave skillfully without tiring.
source: Diné Bahaneʼ; Aileen O'Bryan, The Diné: Origin Myths of the Navajo Indians (1956)

title: Sikakokuh and the Hunting Dog
summary: A Hopi story about a boy named Sikakokuh who had a remarkable hunting dog. The dog was no ordinary animal but possessed spiritual power, able to track game without fail and communicate with its master. Sikakokuh's success as a hunter made others jealous. Through the dog's guidance, Sikakokuh learned the proper rituals for hunting, including prayers and offerings to the spirits of the animals he killed. When enemies or jealous rivals tried to harm the dog, supernatural forces protected both boy and animal. The story teaches the sacred relationship between humans and animals, the importance of gratitude, and the proper conduct of hunters who must respect the spirits of the animals they take.
source: Harold Courlander, People of the Short Blue Corn (1993)

title: Sweet Medicine and the Four Sacred Arrows
summary: Sweet Medicine (Motsé'eóeve) was the great prophet and law-giver of the Cheyenne people. He journeyed to Nóávóse (Bear Butte) in the Black Hills where Ma'heo'o (the Wise One Above) gave him the four sacred arrows—the Maahótse. Two arrows were for hunting, to ensure success in finding buffalo, and two were for warfare, to protect the people from enemies. Sweet Medicine also received the structure of Cheyenne government, the Council of Forty-Four peace chiefs, the military societies, and the system of legal justice. He predicted the coming of horses, white men, and the loss of the buffalo. He also invented the hoop-and-stick game (chunkey).

title: Skunk Outwits Coyote
summary: Coyote came upon a prairie dog village and decided to catch himself a meal. He blocked all the holes except one and waited with his mouth open at the entrance. But Skunk saw what Coyote was doing and decided to teach him a lesson. Skunk approached Coyote and asked what he was doing. Coyote explained his plan, and Skunk offered to help by driving the prairie dogs toward Coyote's mouth. Instead, Skunk walked up to Coyote and sprayed him directly in the face. Coyote ran away howling, and the prairie dogs escaped. Skunk laughed at the trickster, proving that even the cleverest schemer can be outsmarted.

title: Splinter Foot Girl
summary: A childless Arapaho woman found a splinter embedded in her foot. To her amazement, the splinter grew and transformed into a baby girl. The girl grew rapidly and possessed supernatural abilities. She could communicate with animals and had the power to heal. She brought good fortune to her family and community, helping them find buffalo and warning them of danger. One day, a rival tribe kidnapped her, but she used her powers to escape and return home. The story teaches that blessings can come from unexpected places, and that even something as small as a splinter can hold great potential.

title: Shakuru and Pah: The Sun and Moon
summary: Shakuru (the Sun) and Pah (the Moon) were the last of all gods placed in the heavens by Tirawa. They are of relatively minor standing in Skidi Pawnee mythology compared to the star gods. The Sun and Moon produced a son, a boy who was placed on Earth to be the ancestor of the Pawnee people. The Sun is associated with warmth, light, and the daily cycle. The Moon governs the night and is associated with women's cycles. In Pawnee earth lodge construction, the door faces east to welcome the rising sun, and the smoke hole is aligned to observe the moon and stars.

title: Spearfinger (U'tlun'ta): The Stone Witch (Cherokee)
summary: Spearfinger, also known as Nûñ'yunu'ï or 'Stone-dress,' is a fearsome Cherokee monster and witch who lives along the eastern side of Tennessee and western North Carolina. She has a long, sharp forefinger on her right hand like a spear or obsidian knife, which she uses to cut open her victims. She wears a dress made of stone that makes her nearly invulnerable. Spearfinger feeds on human livers, especially those of children. Her mouth is perpetually stained with blood. She can mimic the voices of loved ones to lure victims close. According to legend, she was eventually defeated by a group of Cherokee warriors who discovered that her only vulnerable spot was a small point on her palm. They shot arrows into her palm, and she fell from the mountain where she lived.
source: Wikipedia - Cherokee spiritual beliefs; Sacred Texts - U'tlun'ta

title: Stone Coat (Nûñ'yunu'ï): The Rock Giants (Cherokee)
summary: Stone Coat, also called Nûñ'yunu'ï, is a race of giant beings covered in stone-like skin that makes them nearly invulnerable. They dwell in the remote mountains and caves of Cherokee territory. These rock giants are cannibalistic and prey upon humans who venture too far into their domain. They are incredibly strong, capable of throwing boulders and tearing down trees. Stone Coat is also associated with Spearfinger, who wears a stone dress. The giants' only weakness is their vulnerability to certain spiritual practices. Cherokee medicine men knew rituals to confuse or drive away Stone Coats. The legend may have been inspired by encounters with the fossilized remains of prehistoric megafauna in the Appalachian region, and it serves as a warning against venturing into dangerous, unknown territory.
source: Wikipedia - Cherokee spiritual beliefs; native-languages.org Stoneclad

title: Sinti Lapitta: The Horned Serpent (Choctaw)
summary: Sinti Lapitta is a great horned serpent from Choctaw mythology, a water monster with immense power. Described as a giant snake with a head like a panther or with two horns on its head, it dwells in deep rivers, lakes, and water holes. Unlike the malevolent horned serpents of some tribes, Sinti Lapitta was sometimes known to visit unusually wise young men, bestowing knowledge and power upon them. The serpent was both feared and respected, embodying the power of the water realm. It served as a symbol of the underworld and was associated with the chaotic forces that must be balanced against the order of the sky. Stories of Sinti Lapitta warn against disturbing sacred waters.

title: Seminole Creation: Breathmaker and the Earth (Seminole)
summary: The Seminole creation story begins with Breathmaker, the great Creator god, who existed before anything else. Breathmaker created the earth and all living things. In the Seminole version of the Muskogean creation tradition, the earth was formed from mud brought up from the primordial waters, similar to the Cherokee story. Breathmaker then created humans, animals, and plants, and taught the Seminole how to live properly. He gave them the Green Corn Ceremony and established the clans. Breathmaker also created the sun, moon, and stars to govern time and seasons. The Seminole believe that Breathmaker remains involved in the world and can be communicated with through prayer and ceremony. The creation story establishes the sacred relationship between the Seminole people and their land in Florida.

title: Stikini: The Owl Witches (Seminole)
summary: Stikini are dreaded owl-witches from Seminole folklore. During the day, Stikini appear as ordinary humans, but at night they transform into monstrous owls with supernatural powers. They are known to prey on humans, particularly those who are alone in the forest at night. A Stikini can be identified by its bloodshot eyes and unusual behavior. The owl-witches are associated with witchcraft and dark magic, and their appearance is considered an omen of death or misfortune. Seminole medicine men have the power to identify Stikini and perform rituals to protect against them. The legend serves as a warning against practicing evil magic and emphasizes the importance of staying within the protection of the community, especially at night.

title: Sint-Holo: The Horned Serpent (Chickasaw)
summary: Sint-Holo is a dragon-like horned serpent from Chickasaw mythology. This powerful water monster dwells in deep rivers, lakes, and underground springs. Sint-Holo has a serpentine body covered with scales, a horned or antlered head, and possesses supernatural powers. Unlike some malevolent serpents in neighboring tribes' mythology, Sint-Holo is sometimes ambivalent or even protective of the Chickasaw people. The serpent is associated with the underground water realm and represents the forces of nature that must be respected. Those who encounter Sint-Holo must show proper respect or face dire consequences. The horned serpent symbol appears in Chickasaw art and ceremonial objects and is one of the most widespread mythological figures across Southeastern Native American cultures.

title: Sisiutl the Double-Headed Serpent (Kwakwaka'wakw)
summary: The Sisiutl is a fearsome three-headed sea serpent whose glance can turn an adversary into stone. Typically depicted as a double-headed serpent with a central humanoid face representing Baxbaxwalanuksiwe' or Komokwa, it has horned heads, protruding tongues, and spiral noses that indicate supernatural power. The Sisiutl is closely associated with shamans, who mediate between natural and supernatural worlds, and warriors believed that its blood made them invincible in battle. It appears as a protective figure on clan house beams, war canoes, and ceremonial regalia. In some myths, the Sisiutl transforms into an invincible war canoe or a magic belt that protects the wearer against all dangers. The beach mineral mica was believed to be the scales of the Sisiutl and was incorporated into ceremonial traditions. The Sisiutl plays a dual role as both a terrifying monster and a powerful helper to ancestors who earned its favor.
source: Kwakwaka'wakw oral tradition; Franz Boas; Wikipedia

title: Stagolee Shoots Billy Lyons
summary: On Christmas night, 1895, in a St. Louis saloon, the notorious bad-man Stagolee (based on historical figure Lee Shelton) shot William 'Billy' Lyons after a dispute. In the folk tradition, the argument began when Billy Lyons snatched Stagolee's prized Stetson hat from his head and refused to return it. Stagolee warned him: 'I don't care what you do with me, but don't you mess with my Stetson hat.' When Billy laughed and refused, Stagolee drew his .44 pistol. Billy begged for his life, pleading that he had a wife and children, but Stagolee replied, 'I don't care about your wife and kids—you should have thought of them before you touched my Stetson hat.' He shot Billy dead. The song spread like wildfire across America, with each new version making Stagolee more mythic, transforming a common barroom murder into a foundational American outlaw legend—the quintessential 'bad nigger' who answered to no man and no law.
source: Historical record (St. Louis Globe-Democrat, 1895); snip African American folk ballad tradition

title: Stagolee in Hell (Stagolee vs. the Devil)
summary: After Stagolee was hanged for murder, he arrived at the gates of Hell, still wearing his Stetson hat and carrying his .44 pistol. When the Devil tried to send him to his eternal punishment, Stagolee refused to submit. He pulled his pistol and challenged the Devil himself. The Devil, for all his power, found he could not control Stagolee. Stagolee kicked the Devil off his throne, sat down on it himself, and took over the running of Hell. In some versions, he set all the lost souls free; snip in others, he ran Hell more efficiently than the Devil ever did. The Devil eventually begged God to let Stagolee into Heaven, but Heaven refused as well, leaving Stagolee master of his own domain. This tale of a man so bad that Hell itself could not hold him became a cornerstone of African American 'toasts' (narrative poems), celebrating the ultimate rebellious figure who fears neither God nor Devil and answers to absolutely no one.
source: African American toast tradition; snip Julius Lester, Black Folktales (1969); snip folk ballad variants

title: Stagolee's Funeral
summary: The folk ballad describes Stagolee's funeral as an event unlike any before seen. Thousands of people came from miles around to see the baddest man who ever lived laid to rest. A procession of mourners stretched for blocks—the women weeping, the men silent and respectful. His coffin was reportedly so fine it cost more than most men earned in a year. The ballad describes the funeral train, the flowers, the hymns sung over his grave. Yet even in death, Stagolee remained defiant. The women who heard of his death 'could not stay at home,' rushing to the funeral in droves. Verses describe how 'the women in the West' heard the news and lamented, cementing Stagolee's status as a tragic romantic figure. Stagolee's funeral became a set-piece in African American folk memory: the moment when the community paused to honor a man who, for all his violence, represented a kind of freedom—the freedom to be utterly oneself, without apology or compromise, in a world that demanded constant submission from Black men.
source: African American folk ballad 'Stagger Lee' (multiple collected variants)

title: Santa Muerte — Holy Death
summary: Santa Muerte (Holy Death or Saint Death) is a female folk saint and personification of death venerated primarily in Mexico and the US Southwest, with an estimated 12 million followers worldwide. She is depicted as a female skeleton wearing a long robe and carrying a scythe and a globe. Her origins blend pre-Columbian death deities such as the Aztec goddess Mictecacihuatl (Lady of the Dead) with the Spanish Grim Reaper (la Parca). Devotees pray to Santa Muerte for protection, healing, financial prosperity, love, and safe passage to the afterlife. Her statues are dressed in different colored robes depending on the petition: white for purification, red for love, gold for money, black for protection against enemies, green for justice. Though condemned by the Catholic Church, Santa Muerte's following exploded in the early 2000s and spread rapidly to the United States through Mexican immigration. Her shrines are found in botanicas and homes throughout Los Angeles, Houston, Chicago, New York, and other cities with large Mexican-American populations. She has particular appeal among those living on society's margins — taxi drivers, sex workers, police, soldiers, and prisoners — who face death's uncertainty daily.
source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Santa_Muerte; Chesnut, R. Andrew 'Devoted to Death: Santa Muerte, the Skeleton Saint' (2018)

title: Shango and the Dual Death (Yoruba)
summary: Shango was the fourth king of Oyo who deified himself as the god of thunder and lightning. According to legend, Shango was a powerful but tyrannical ruler who accidentally destroyed his own palace with lightning when his enemies provoked him. Overcome with grief and shame, he fled Oyo and hanged himself from an ayan tree. But rather than dying, Shango ascended to heaven and became an Orisha of immense power. His two wives, Oshun and Oya (who became the goddess of wind and death), followed him into the spirit world. Shango's sacred symbol is the oshe (double-headed axe), representing swift divine justice. His followers revere him for his fiery temper, his drumming, and his power to hurl thunderbolts at his enemies.

title: Simbi: The River Spirits of the Kongo
summary: The Simbi are powerful water spirits who inhabit the rivers, streams, and sacred pools of the Kongo region. They are ancient beings who existed before humanity and witnessed the creation of the world. Simbi can appear as beautiful human figures, snakes, or shimmering reflections on the water's surface. They are guardians of esoteric knowledge and the natural balance of the forests and waters. Those who respect the Simbi and make proper offerings may receive visions, healing, or supernatural abilities. But those who pollute the waters or hunt excessively incur the Simbi's wrath — they may be dragged into the depths, never to be seen again, or afflicted with madness. The nganga (spiritual specialists) serve as intermediaries between the human world and the Simbi, conducting ceremonies at sacred river sites. The Simbi remind the Kongo people that water is not merely a resource but a living, spiritual force that sustains all existence.
source: Kongo oral tradition, Kongo religion studies
moral: The natural world is animated by spirits who must be respected and honored.

title: Silamaka and Pullori: The Fulani Epic Heroes
summary: The Fulani epic of Silamaka and Pullori recounts the adventures of two bound-by-friendship heroes from the Macina region of Mali. Silamaka, the saber-wielding warrior, is challenged to prove his temerity against the mighty Bambara king Da Monzon Diarra. He consults a geomancer who tells him that to become invulnerable, he must capture alive the sacred serpent Galamani that prowls the forest. With Pullori's help, Silamaka succeeds where many had failed, capturing the serpent and fashioning a protective belt. His fame spreads across Fulani villages. Da Monzon counterattacks with powerful magic, but Pullori, armed with Silamaka's enchanted lance, flees with their sons. As night falls, they all disappear, ascending to heaven. The epic symbolizes Fulani resistance against Bambara domination and remains a cornerstone of Fulani oral tradition.

title: Sina and the Eel (Origin of the Coconut)
summary: The beautiful maiden Sina drew water from a pool where she discovered a young eel. She took it home as a pet and named it Tuna. The eel grew rapidly, outgrowing bowls, then pools, and becoming unnaturally large. Wherever Sina bathed, the eel appeared and encircled her. Frightened, she fled across multiple islands, but the eel always found her. Finally, the eel revealed itself as the King of Fiji transformed by magic, who confessed his love and asked Sina to bury his head. Though shocked, Sina complied. From the buried head grew the first coconut tree, its three dark pores representing the eel's eyes and mouth. When cracked, the coconut's shell resembles the eel's face.

title: Semetey: The Son of Manas
summary: After Manas's death, his treacherous younger brothers Abyke and Bobosh seized power and tried to kill Manas's son Semetey. Kanykey, Manas's widow, escaped with the infant Semetey to Bukhara, where her father ruled as khan. Semetey grew up unaware of his heritage until his mother revealed his identity. He returned to the Talas valley on the magical horse Taytoru to avenge his father's death. Semetey defeated his treacherous uncles in battle and beheaded the forty companions of his father who had betrayed Manas. He married the beautiful Ay-Chürök, a swan maiden, and continued his father's legacy of defending the Kyrgyz people. The second part of the Manas trilogy bears his name.

title: Shurale the Forest Spirit
summary: Shurale is the most famous mythological creature in Tatar folklore — a forest spirit depicted as a hairy man with a horn on his forehead, one eye, and extremely long fingers. Shurale lures travelers into the forest, rides their horses at night, and tickles his victims to death. The most famous story, immortalized by Tatar poet Gabdulla Tukay, tells of a young woodcutter who outwits Shurale by tricking him into inserting his fingers into a split log, then removing the wedge to trap him. When Shurale screams in pain, other forest spirits ask what happened, and he tells them 'Byltyr kysty' (Last year pinched me) — so afraid of admitting he was fooled. The story teaches cleverness over brute strength and remains a cornerstone of Tatar cultural identity.

title: Su Anasi: The Water Mother
summary: Su Anasi ('Mother of Water') is the Tatar water spirit, analogous to the Russian rusalka but with distinct Tatar characteristics. Long-haired and beautiful, she lives in rivers and lakes, with a golden comb that she uses to brush her hair while sitting on riverbanks. The most famous version, retold by poet Gabdulla Tukay, describes a village boy who finds Su Anasi's golden comb by the river and runs away with it. The water mother chases him all the way to the village until the village dogs drive her away. That night, she comes to his window, tapping and demanding her comb back. The boy's mother throws the comb out the window to appease her. The story teaches children not to take things that don't belong to them, as every object has a spirit owner ('iyäsi') who will reclaim it.

title: Sodani the Great Shaman
summary: The Evenki epic tale of Sodani, the most powerful shaman who ever lived, who could journey between the Upper, Middle, and Lower Worlds. The story follows Sodani's initiation by spirits, his epic journey to the Lower World to retrieve a lost soul, and his battle against hostile spirits that threaten the Evenki people. He is aided by his animal spirit helpers including the reindeer, bear, and eagle. The tale illustrates the Evenki shamanic worldview where humans are but a small piece of the natural world and must maintain balance with all beings — animals, plants, rivers, mountains, and stars all have spirits that must be respected for harmony to prevail.

title: Songtsen Gampo and the Founding of Tibet
summary: The semi-legendary account of King Songtsen Gampo (7th century CE), the founder of the Tibetan Empire and the first Dharma King. According to Tibetan tradition, he was an emanation of the bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara, sent to bring civilization and Buddhism to the Tibetan people. He built the Jokhang Temple in Lhasa, subdued the demoness that lay beneath the Tibetan landscape by constructing 108 temples on her body's pressure points, and invented the Tibetan script by sending his minister Thonmi Sambhota to India. His marriage to Princess Wencheng of China and Princess Bhrikuti of Nepal is said to have brought Buddhism to Tibet. The story of Songtsen Gampo blends historical events with elaborate mythological elements that define Tibetan Buddhist identity.

title: Sri Thanonchai: The Royal Trickster
summary: Sri Thanonchai is the legendary trickster figure in Thai folklore, a clever and witty commoner who used his sharp intellect to outsmart kings, nobles, and corrupt officials. Born with extraordinary cleverness, Sri Thanonchai could talk his way out of any situation and often turned the tables on those in power who sought to harm him. His stories, passed down through generations, involve elaborate wordplay, cunning schemes, and moral lessons about the power of wit over brute force. In one famous tale, he tricked a greedy official into believing a common pot was a magical fortune-telling device. Despite his mischief, Sri Thanonchai never used his tricks to harm the innocent — only to expose greed, hypocrisy, and abuse of power. His tales remain widely told in Thai oral tradition and have been adapted into television dramas, plays, and children's books.
moral: Wit and intelligence are more powerful than wealth or status, but cleverness should serve justice, not greed.
source: Thai folklore, oral tradition, Thai literature

title: Sangkuriang and Mount Tangkuban Perahu
summary: A Sundanese legend from West Java telling of Sangkuriang, a young man who unknowingly falls in love with his mother Dayang Sumbi, a goddess-blessed princess who remains eternally young. When Dayang Sumbi recognizes her son by a scar, she sets impossible tasks to prevent their marriage: he must dam the Citarum River and build a lake with a boat before sunrise. With supernatural help, Sangkuriang nearly succeeds, but Dayang Sumbi tricks him by lighting the eastern sky with red cloth to mimic dawn. Enraged at the deception, Sangkuriang kicks the unfinished boat, which forms Mount Tangkuban Perahu (Upturned Boat), a volcanic mountain near Bandung.
notes: Classic Oedipus-complex narrative. Tangkuban Perahu is an active volcano near Bandung.
moral: The natural and spiritual world reveal divine truths to those who seek them.

title: Sang Kancil and the Crocodile
summary: The most famous fable from the Malay Archipelago features Sang Kancil, the clever mouse-deer, outsmarting a river full of hungry crocodiles. Wanting to cross the river to reach the fruit trees on the other side, Kancil tells the crocodiles that the king has ordered a headcount of all crocodiles, instructing them to line up across the river. The crocodiles oblige, and Kancil hops across their backs to the far shore. When the crocodiles realize they have been tricked, Kancil is already enjoying the fruits of victory. This tale, deeply embedded in Malay culture, celebrates wit over physical strength and appears in the coat of arms of Malacca with two mouse-deer supporting the shield.
notes: Sang Kancil is the archetypal trickster hero in Malay folklore, analogous to Br'er Rabbit.
moral: Wit and cleverness triumph over brute strength and arrogance.

title: Si Tanggang (The Unfilial Son)
summary: A Malaysian folktale from Negeri Sembilan closely related to the Indonesian Malin Kundang. Tanggang, the son of a poor fisherman, leaves his mother to seek fortune overseas. After becoming a wealthy captain with a fine ship, he returns to his village. His elderly mother, overjoyed, wades into the water to greet him, but Tanggang denies knowing her, ashamed of her poverty-stricken appearance. His mother curses him for his ingratitude. A violent storm erupts, and Tanggang and his ship are petrified into stone. The rock formation known as Batu Belah Batu Bertangkup (Split Stone, Closing Stone) is said to be the remains of his ship, serving as a warning against filial disrespect in Malay culture.
notes: A variant of the widespread 'unfilial son' motif found across Maritime Southeast Asia.
moral: Honor your parents, for ingratitude brings divine punishment.

title: Si Bungsu and the Seven Brothers
summary: A traditional Bruneian folktale about the youngest of seven brothers who was underestimated and mistreated by his six elder siblings. While the elder brothers were strong, skilled, and proud, Si Bungsu (meaning 'the youngest') was gentle, humble, and assigned the most menial tasks. When the Sultan announced a challenge to retrieve a sacred kris (ceremonial blade) lost in the enchanted forest, the elder brothers set off confidently but failed — their arrogance angering the forest's guardian spirit. Si Bungsu, guided by humility and compassion, helped an old woman (the disguised forest spirit), who rewarded him with the kris and freed his spellbound brothers. He shared his reward with them, teaching that true strength lies not in skill but in kindness and compassion.
notes: Classic 'youngest child prevails' narrative. Emphasizes Bruneian values of humility and family unity.
moral: Kindness is always rewarded while cruelty brings its own destruction.

title: Samudra Manthan — The Churning of the Ocean
summary: After sage Durvasa cursed Indra and the gods to lose all their strength, the demons (asuras) took over the universe. Lord Vishnu advised the gods to churn the Ocean of Milk with the demons to obtain amrita, the nectar of immortality. They used Mount Mandara as the churning rod and the serpent Vasuki as the rope. However, the mountain began to sink, so Vishnu took his Kurma (tortoise) avatar to support it on his back. As they churned, fourteen treasures emerged, including the deadly poison halahala, which Shiva drank to save creation, turning his throat blue (earning him the name Nilakantha). Finally, Dhanvantari emerged with the pot of nectar. Vishnu then took the form of Mohini to distract the demons and ensure only the gods received immortality.
source: Bhagavata Purana, Vishnu Purana, Mahabharata (Adi Parva)
moral: Wisdom and wit prevail where strength alone cannot

title: Savitri and Satyavan — Love That Conquered Death
summary: Princess Savitri chose to marry Satyavan despite the sage Narada's warning that he was destined to die within a year. On the fated day, Yama, the god of death, came to claim Satyavan's soul. As Yama carried the soul away, Savitri followed him through the wilderness. Impressed by her devotion, Yama granted her three boons — but not Satyavan's life. Savitri asked for her father-in-law's eyesight and kingdom to be restored, and then for a hundred sons for herself. When Yama granted the third boon, Savitri pointed out that she could not bear children without her husband. Bound by his word, Yama was forced to restore Satyavan to life. The story, from the Mahabharata, is a profound testament that love, wit, and determination can overcome even death.
moral: Love transcends death but society's cruelty destroys it

title: Sohni Mahiwal — Love Across the River
summary: Sohni, a beautiful pottery-maker's daughter, fell in love with Izzat Baig (later called Mahiwal), a wealthy merchant from another land who had come to trade. When her family married her to another man, Sohni still swam each night across the Chenab River using an earthenware pot as a float to reach her beloved Mahiwal on the opposite bank. Her sister-in-law, suspecting the affair, replaced Sohni's baked clay pot with an unbaked one. When Sohni jumped into the river, the pot dissolved in the water, and she drowned in the swirling currents. Mahiwal, seeing her body float downstream, jumped in and died beside her. One of the four classic tragic romances of Punjab, the story symbolizes love that defies social boundaries but meets a tragic end.
source: Punjabi-Sindhi oral folklore, Soomra dynasty period (10th century)
moral: Love transcends death but society's cruelty destroys it

title: Sassi Punnun — Searching Across the Desert
summary: Sassi, a princess born to the king of Bhambore but abandoned at birth due to an astrological prophecy, was raised by a washerman. She grew into a woman of extraordinary beauty. Hearing of her, Punnun, a Baloch prince, traveled to Bhambore and fell in love with her at first sight. They married in secret, but Punnun's jealous brothers drugged him and carried him away across the desert while he slept. Waking to find her beloved gone, Sassi ran barefoot through the scorching Thar Desert, calling his name. Her delicate feet blistered and bled, but she pressed on until she collapsed and died of exhaustion. Punnun, upon waking and realizing what had happened, raced back toward her. Finding her body, he died of grief beside her. Their graves became a shrine for lovers in Sindh.
moral: Love transcends death but society's cruelty destroys it

title: Santhal Creation Myth — The World Born from Water
summary: The Santhal tribe, one of India's largest Adivasi communities, tells that in the beginning, the world was covered entirely with water. The supreme being Thakur Jiu (or Singbonga) sent the divine crow Marangbonga to find earth. The crow tried but found only water. Next, a tortoise and a crab attempted but failed. Finally, an earthworm brought up mud from the bottom of the primordial ocean. Thakur Jiu molded this mud into the first land. He then created the first human couple, Pilcu Haram and Pilcu Budhi, from clay. But they were imperfect, so he tried seven more times. The eighth attempt succeeded, but the couple was afraid of the cosmic darkness. Thakur Jiu then created the sun, moon, trees, and animals to accompany them. The bonga spirits, both benevolent and mischievous, inhabit all aspects of nature and must be honored through rituals led by the village shaman.
source: Santhal oral traditions, documented by P.O. Bodding and missionary Diku Skrefsrud (1887)
moral: Wisdom and wit prevail where strength alone cannot

title: Sinbad the Sailor (Seven Voyages)
summary: A wealthy Baghdad merchant known as Sinbad the Sailor recounts his seven extraordinary voyages to a poor porter named Sinbad the Landsman. Each voyage brings shipwreck, monstrous creatures (the giant bird Roc, gigantic serpents, whale-islands, cyclopean giants), and fantastic lands. Sinbad outwits cannibals, escapes the Valley of Diamonds, rides sea monsters, and encounters the Old Man of the Sea who clings to his shoulders. Through wit, courage, and Allah's mercy, he returns home each time richer, only to be driven again by wanderlust. The tales are a masterpiece of Arab maritime folklore.

title: Simurgh and Zal
summary: Sam of Zabulistan abandons his albino infant son Zal on Mount Alborz, ashamed of his white hair. The mythical bird Simurgh, a wise, benevolent creature resembling a giant dog-bird, finds the baby and raises him as her own. Zal grows into a handsome, powerful youth. Sam has a dream of reproach and seeks forgiveness. Zal returns to his father but receives three Simurgh feathers to burn when in need. Later, Zal marries the beautiful Rudabeh, but she nearly dies in childbirth. Zal burns a feather, and Simurgh appears to perform the first known cesarean section, saving both mother and child — the child being Rostam, Iran's greatest hero.

title: Siyavash and Sudabeh
summary: Prince Siyavash, son of Kay Kavus, rejects the seductive advances of his stepmother Sudabeh. To prove his innocence, he rides through a mountain of fire and emerges unscathed. Despite this vindication, he cannot remain in Iran and voluntarily exiles himself to Turan, where King Afrasiab initially welcomes him and gives him his daughter Farangis in marriage. Suspecting conspiracy, Afrasiab orders Siyavash's execution, and the prince is beheaded. This unjust death becomes the casus belli for generations of war between Iran and Turan, and Siyavash's blood is avenged by his son Kay Khosrow. The story is one of the Shahnameh's most profound tragedies, exploring honor, betrayal, and fate.

title: Sufax and the Founding of Tangier
summary: In the syncretic mythology that blends Berber, Greek, and Phoenician traditions, Sufax was the son of the Greek hero Heracles (Hercules) and Tinge, the Berber earth goddess. According to the legend, Heracles performed one of his labors in North Africa, where he met and married Tinge. Their son Sufax founded the city of Tangier, naming it after his mother (Tingis being the ancient name for Tangier). Sufax is also credited with founding parts of Mauritania. This myth demonstrates the cultural fusion characteristic of North African mythology, where Berber indigenous figures merged with Greek heroes from the time of the Greek colonies in Cyrenaica, and later with Roman interpretations. The story explains the founding of one of Morocco's oldest cities and illustrates how Berber mythology absorbed and transformed external influences while maintaining its indigenous core.
